items.minimax_notes: v3 with reasoning OFF: response terminates cleanly at 3649 tokens (vs v2's 6000 truncation) and ships a structurally complete file with tests. BUT the code DOES NOT COMPILE for multiple reasons: (1) `Shard<K,V>` struct is declared with fields {map, lru, next_pos} — NO `rwlock` field — yet every method calls `shard.rwlock.read()` / `.write()`. Compile error on first method body. (2) `Shard` has no `capacity` field, but `put` calls `guard.capacity`. (3) `touch::<K2,V2>` declares `K2: Eq` only, then calls `key.clone()` and uses `K2` inside `Shard<K2,V2>` (which requires Hash) — bound errors. (4) `evict_if` calls `self.shard(&pred)` passing a closure where `K` is expected — type error. (5) `all_shards` is moved into the first for-loop, then re-iterated in the second loop after move — borrow-checker error. (6) `evict_if` explicitly returns 0 placeholder, admitting 'count is omitted for brevity.' (7) `get` does NOT promote recency — just clones from map. Direct LRU semantics violation (rubric #2). (8) `put` updates value AND calls `Self::touch(&mut guard, &key, *pos)` but `touch` does the deque scan O(n). (9) `next_pos` field is incremented but never used to drive O(1) ops — it's dead weight (same as v2). POSITIVES vs v2: structural completeness, three named tests including a concurrent thread::scope-ish (actually plain spawn) test, no truncation, design rationale is coherent in prose. But the prose claims things the code does not deliver: 'No data races – every field is protected by RwLock' is false because Shard has no RwLock at all in the struct definition. Less verbose deliberation but the dropped reasoning means the model didn't catch that it forgot to wrap Shard in RwLock — a fatal architectural oversight reasoning would have caught.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2: striped design with UnsafeCell layout-cast UB, empty stub list ops 'omitted for brevity', test exists but checks nothing meaningful. Compiles though.
items.key_diff: v3 minimax ships more code but with a brain-dead bug (missing rwlock field) that any compile-check would catch — the 'no reasoning' tradeoff bites hard here. DSV4 still wins on shipping-something-that-compiles.

items.minimax_notes: v3 terminates at 1582 tokens (vs v2's larger). Includes proper rubric-touching: handles rectangular case explicitly, raises ValueError on infeasibility, uses separate `way`/`column_of_min` array (an improvement over v2 which conflated p[] and way[]). BUT still broken: (1) `minv` array is initialized to 0.0 instead of INF — the relaxation `if cur_cost < minv[j]` only triggers when reduced cost is negative, so the algorithm never properly finds the min for unvisited columns. This breaks correctness on most non-trivial inputs. (2) The square-matrix-via-dummy-rows scheme is described in comments but never actually implemented as a separate matrix — the dummy-row handling is inline (`if i0 > n: cur_cost = -u[i0] - v[j]`), which technically works but the outer loop only iterates `for i in range(1, n+1)` — it NEVER processes dummy rows (i > n). So the augmenting paths can't use dummies to cover impossible-to-match real workers. (3) Augmenting path: `while True: j1 = column_of_min[j0]; p[j0] = p[j1]; if j0==0: break; j0 = j1` — terminates only when j0 == 0. The initial j0 ends at some j1 where p[j1]==0, but the loop reads column_of_min[j0] which during relaxation was set to PRIOR j0 values. Will traverse back through column_of_min chain. Order-of-operations bug: assigns `p[j0]=p[j1]` BEFORE check, so on first iter when j0!=0, it correctly assigns. But the chain may not reach 0 cleanly. (4) Inf handling: at the very end checks `if costs[i][job] == INF: raise infeasible` — that handles all-inf rows, but during relaxation `cur_cost = costs[i0-1][j-1] - u[i0] - v[j]` produces `inf - finite = inf`, and `inf < delta(INF)` is False, so inf edges are skipped — that part is right. (5) Reduced-cost computation uses raw costs[i0-1][j-1] each iteration — that's the O(n^3) Hungarian, correct shape. POSITIVES vs v2: separate `way`/`column_of_min` is the right idea, infeasibility check at the end is correct in shape. The MAIN bug (`minv` not init to INF) is a textbook reasoning miss — exactly the kind of thing reasoning would have caught.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2: correct 1-indexed O(n^3) Hungarian, terminates at 1043 tokens.
items.key_diff: minimax shows architectural improvement (separated way[] array) but blows it with `minv` not initialized to infinity. DSV4 unchanged, still works. DSV4 +3.

items.minimax_notes: v3 at 1908 tokens. NET REGRESSION vs v2 on the most important rubric item: (1) RUBRIC #2 SAYS use redis.call('TIME') inside the script and do NOT pass client time as authoritative. v3 calls `this.redis.time()` from the TypeScript client, then passes `nowMs` as ARGV[1] — exact violation of the rubric. v2 used redis.call('TIME') inside the Lua script (correct). v3 regressed here, likely because reasoning-off skipped a sanity-check pass. (2) `runScriptWithoutAdd` passed `this.scriptSha` as the sha for the READ_ONLY_LUA script — but scriptSha was set from the WRITE LUA_SCRIPT! `remaining` and `resetAt` will execute the WRITE script (which adds entries) — silent mutation on read APIs. Major bug. (3) `math.random()` without a seed is also not great but minor. (4) EXPIRE is now present (improvement vs v2 missing it) — good. (5) ZADD uses `now..':'..math.random()` for uniqueness — fine. (6) resetAt correctly computes `oldestTs + windowSec*1000` — better than DSV4's `now+window`. (7) TypeScript types: AllowResult-style interfaces present, no `any` in returns. (8) Correctness argument is 4 sentences as required, addresses the prune-before-count ordering. POSITIVES: EXPIRE added, oldest-based resetAt, fewer collisions on member uniqueness. NEGATIVES: client-time violation (rubric #2 hard miss), remaining/resetAt silently mutate. The losses cancel the wins. Down 1 from v2.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2: uses now-seconds as member, no EXPIRE, resetAt non-atomic and returns now+window unconditionally.
items.key_diff: v3 minimax has a worse rubric profile than v2 minimax (lost the redis.call('TIME') compliance, introduced silent-mutation bug in read APIs). DSV4 unchanged and now narrowly wins.

items.minimax_notes: v3 hit length=6000 again, but DRAMATICALLY more useful truncation than v2. Truncation happens in the test-harness summary printf at the very end — the actual `json_extract_int`, `follow_path`, `parse_value`, `parse_number`, `parse_string`, `parse_literal`, `skip_ws` ALL exist and are syntactically complete (closing braces present). 20 test cases declared (vs v2 which had ZERO definition of the target function). This is the biggest v3 improvement: the model spent budget on actual code rather than deliberation. BUGS: (1) parse_number rejects INT64_MIN: parse_number parses `9223372036854775808` (digit-by-digit) and overflows on the last digit because INT64_MAX is 9223372036854775807. So test #19 (-9223372036854775808) will FAIL — the sign is consumed first, then the magnitude parse overflows before negation. The code has a post-negate overflow check `if (val > ((int64_t)0 - INT64_MIN)/2)` which is wrong-headed but never reached because the digit-parse overflow trips first. (2) parse_value for object/array always returns JERR_TYPE_MISMATCH after consuming, even if traversal succeeded — this is fine because follow_path does its own object walking, not relying on parse_value for traversal. (3) parse_value for object: when value isn't an integer (e.g., a nested object), recursive parse_value(&q, out) is called with target=out — that means a stray integer inside a string-valued field would overwrite *out. Actually re-reading: parse_value writes to *out for numbers, but for strings/objects it returns JERR_TYPE_MISMATCH WITHOUT writing. Fine. (4) Whitespace check in parse_literal misses U+00A0-style non-breaking space — but ASCII-only is rubric-compliant. (5) skip_ws doesn't bound-check explicitly but '\0' terminates it correctly. (6) Bracket-mismatch detection: if input is `[1,2,3` (unterminated), parse_value for array enters for-loop, parses 1, sees ',', continues, parses 2, sees ',', parses 3, sees '\0' on skip_ws which returns JERR_END_OF_INPUT. Caught. (7) Path parsing handles `.` and `[N]`, name limited to 63 chars. The `else if (*path_p != '\0')` 'implicit dot' branch is unreachable / falls through awkwardly but doesn't crash because the top-of-loop checks for valid first char. (8) 8+ test cases delivered: 20 declared. (9) The 'argument for not reading past null terminator' is provided in the comment header as required. POSITIVES: A real, comprehensive parser implementation. NEGATIVES: INT64_MIN bug, length-truncated printf summary (but core code intact).
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2: signature with `return 1; // simplified` placeholder body. Has test-case list and null-terminator argument as required text.
items.key_diff: v3 minimax flips the matchup: it actually ships a working (modulo INT64_MIN) parser with 20 tests; DSV4 still ships a placeholder. Big win for v3 minimax — first clear B4 win in any version.

items.minimax_notes: v3 at 936 tokens (very tight — best brevity of any item). Identifies 3 bugs. CRITICAL HIT: Bug 3 is `id(object())` non-uniqueness — EXACTLY rubric item #3 (the v2 minimax MISSED this; v3 nails it). MISSES vs rubric: Bug 1 about Queue maxsize is fabricated (queue starts at N and put_nowait happens only after get(), so it's never full); the real bug there is the wait_for cancellation race. Bug 2 about gather(return_exceptions=True) is real but not in rubric. The model doesn't identify the asyncio.wait_for cancellation race (rubric #1 and #2). The corrected version doesn't use asyncio.timeout() or shield(), so the cancellation race is unfixed. CORRECTLY addresses 'no locks needed' (rubric #5) with the right reason (single-threaded asyncio). So: 2 of 5 rubric items hit (Bug 3 + locks question). The 'fix' for Bug 3 uses `id(self)` (stable but identical for all conns — wrong; just hides the bug). POSITIVES vs v2: hit rubric Bug 3 (id(object())) which v2 entirely missed; concise, terminates cleanly; correct lock conclusion. NEGATIVES: misses the cancellation race entirely (which both v1 and v2 also missed, but v2 at least gestured at wait_for); fabricates two bugs.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2: invents 'maxsize but items never removed' (wrong fix), double-returns conn in except + finally. Misses all rubric bugs.
items.key_diff: v3 minimax decisively wins B5 — identifies one rubric bug exactly (id(object())) that v2 missed, correct lock answer, concise. DSV4 still confused.

items.minimax_notes: v3 at 2145 tokens — terminates cleanly this time (vs v2 truncation). Substantially complete: BoundedWorkerPool struct, Submit, runDispatcher, runWorker, safeCall, Shutdown, waitOrCancel, Stats — all defined with bodies and closing braces. COMPILE ERRORS: (1) `safeCall` uses `fmt.Errorf` but `fmt` is NOT imported (only context, errors, sync, sync/atomic). Hard compile error. (2) `panics` counter is declared and read by Stats() but NEVER incremented anywhere — safeCall returns the panic as an error but doesn't bump p.panics. Field is dead. (3) `for i := range workers` is Go 1.22+ syntax, fine on modern Go. (4) `id int` parameter to runWorker is unused (warning, not error). DESIGN ISSUES: (5) Two-channel design (in→out via dispatcher) is overkill and creates ordering complexity. (6) Shutdown does `close(p.in)` inside stopOnce.Do, but dispatcher may ALSO `close(p.in)` in its stopCtx branch if Shutdown's stopFn runs first — wait, actually Shutdown calls stopFn AFTER waiting for stopWg. The dispatcher will see closed-in first (case `j, ok := <-p.in` with !ok) and exit via the !ok branch which `ranges` over p.in (already closed, immediately exits) and closes p.out. So double-close avoided. OK. (7) Submit fast-path has a TOCTOU race: checks `p.stopped.Load()` then sends to p.in — but Shutdown may close(p.in) between check and send → panic on send-to-closed-channel. Real race that needs a mutex or different protocol. (8) RUBRIC #4 (Stats consistency): minimax argues x86-64 atomic reads aren't torn — that's the WRONG direction. The rubric asks why a SEQUENCE of atomic.LoadInt64 calls would produce a torn SNAPSHOT (each read atomic but counters move between reads). Minimax denies the problem exists. Rubric miss. (9) Genuine non-blocking Submit (select+default): YES, correct (rubric #1). (10) Per-task panic recovery via deferred recover in safeCall: YES, worker keeps running (rubric #2). (11) Shutdown waits via workersWg.Wait inside waitOrCancel which respects ctx: YES (rubric #3). (12) No goroutine leak: workers exit when p.out closes; dispatcher exits when p.in closes; both await waitgroups. Correct (rubric #5). POSITIVES vs v2: ships a complete file with closing braces (v2 truncated mid-shutdown debate), Stats() defined (v2 missing). NEGATIVES: fmt not imported (compile error), panics counter never incremented, Stats consistency argument backwards, Submit race on closed channel.
items.dsv4_notes: Unchanged from v2: real Go code, broken task dispatch slice+chan hybrid, simplified result delivery, compiles though.
items.key_diff: v3 minimax now ships compilable-shaped Go (fix the fmt import + 1-line panics counter and it would compile) with correct rubric hits on 4/5 items. DSV4 also has issues. Materially closer than v2 — tie at 4.

summary.overall_takeaway: v3 (reasoning OFF) moves minimax from 2.83 (v2) to 3.67 — closing the gap with DSV4 from 1.17 points to 0.33 points. The trajectory is v1: 1.0 (no compilable code at all) -> v2: 2.83 (emits code but 3/6 truncated, often buggy) -> v3: 3.67 (5/6 terminate cleanly, real code shipped). Token usage dropped substantially: v2 frequently hit 6000-token cap; v3 totals are 3649/1582/1908/6000/936/2145 — only B4 still hits the cap, and even there the truncation falls on the test summary printf rather than the actual function (v2 truncation was always inside the implementation). The 'less verbose deliberation, more actual code' hypothesis is CONFIRMED for delivery completeness. Wins explicitly traceable to v3: B4 went from 2 -> 5 (now ships a working json_extract_int + 20 tests; v2 had only helpers and no target function); B5 went from 3 -> 4 (now identifies the id(object()) rubric bug exactly, which v2 entirely missed; correct lock answer). The 'no reasoning leading to bugs' worry IS borne out for B1 and B3: B1 ships a Shard struct without the RwLock field it claims to use (a reasoning pass would have caught this trivially) — score stays at 2. B3 REGRESSED (5 -> 4) because v3 lost the redis.call('TIME') compliance that v2 had, and introduced a silent-mutation bug where remaining/resetAt accidentally execute the write-script SHA. So reasoning-off is a net win for length-budget completion (B4, B6) and for hitting the obvious rubric items concisely (B5), but it loses sanity checks that catch trivial type-system and rubric-compliance errors (B1, B3). Wins now 2-3-1 (minimax-dsv4-tie) vs v2's 1-4-1 — minimax doubled its outright wins. Next lever: combine reasoning-on with a target-shape system prompt that forces a final-pass compile-check / rubric-checklist before [FINAL], or run reasoning-on only for the harder items (B1, B2, B3) and reasoning-off for the ones that just need delivery (B4, B5, B6).
